speaker: interviewer
text: Max Stodel. My name is Renee Firestone. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tape number four. Say it again.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Renee Firestone. I'm speaking to Max Stodel. It's March 14, 1995, in Culver City. And we're speaking English. You were telling me that you moved in with your second wife and her sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So We-- I think I moved in with them on March 1946, because they were able to get an apartment that was very hard to get in the Netherlands at that time-- because so many houses were bombed, and so many houses were broken into and used for firewood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so-- Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we moved in. And we couldn't marry at the time on account of that they were not declared dead in 1946-- '45 or '46. Sure enough, my wife had an uncle with a little bit of money in England. And he took a lawyer for us that we were able to get married in 1946, what was in November 20, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got married where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened then? Six weeks later or so my daughter was born. So we wanted to get married before my daughter was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Betty.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still living with your brother-in-law and sister-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, when we got married, my wife and I, we saw to it that we got our own apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing? What kind of work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a presser at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I made a living being a presser. And my wife, was 16 years old when she got her own factory. She made-- she worked also a little bit at home making raincoats. And we both worked hard after the war, since we had nothing to our account. We built up. We had to build up. We had a family then. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] knock on wood, we made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife had her own business?

speaker: interviewee
text: When she was 16 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got married, did she have a business after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1952, we start that same business again. I was not involved in the-- when she was 16 years old. I didn't know her at the time. We had a small factory at the time, in 1952, for a couple, maybe a year or so, till we, in 1954, we started applying to go to United States, which took us almost three years to get our visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in Holland how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: From '45, when I came back, till '56-- May '56.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said that you had a little factory in 1952.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct. Around '52. I don't know if it was '52 or '53. It was something around that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you gave it up before you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't get our lease renewed from the location where we worked. So we had to give it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you give that up? Which year did you give up the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe in a year or so, a year and a half, two years. I don't know how long it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't work in that factory. My wife worked there with her sister, and about three people we had working for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were pressing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was pressing. Not with them. I was working in another company.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then in May '56 you were able to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sister and your brother-in-law come to the States, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: The five us came all at the same time. We were always a family.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went by boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the boat? And where did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hoboken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you settle there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our destination was set by the HIAS-- Trenton, New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about what happened when you

speaker: interviewee
text: In Trenton, I-- when-- before we left Holland, when we were-- we were sponsored by the HIAS, and we needed a-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a job. And we couldn't be at the expense of that-- of the American government for the first five years. So I found work right away. My brother-in-law was struggling. The girls found a job right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a ceramic factory there, and they're very handy, both of them. And they worked in the ceramic factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they did?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a presser. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And like I said, my brother-in-law was struggling. And we had friends living here about two blocks away from here. Living here in Los-- in Culver City. So my brother-in-law and my sister-in-law packed up, and they went here. But that was very disturbing for us, because we, the three of us, my wife, my daughter, and me, were the first time that we were separated. And that they were always together, and they came together, and always together. Naturally we packed up also, in Trenton. Oh, so [INAUDIBLE], all the stuff we had to pack again, and ship, and took it with us. We came by railways, my wife, my daughter, and me, across the United States without decent speaking the language. We left on a Saturday afternoon. A Saturday morning we still were wrapping the bags and the bicycle in the train station. That took a lot of stuff with them on the bus. We crossed the United States. From Saturday afternoon, 4:30 we left Trenton. And we arrived Wednesday morning after the bus broke down in Phoenix-- in Culver-- in Los Angeles. And somebody picked us up, on account of there was a taxi strike at the time. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we came to California you settled in Congress City right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We stayed with my sister-in-law for a few days-- at my brother-in-law. It was the first night we ever slept in a motel, across the street from my sister-in-law's place. And we looked for an apartment. We found an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your daughter then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine years old

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you find a job right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I looked maybe three, four, five days. After a lot of promises, I finally got a job on account of a guy who was always drunk. And my boss tell him, the next you get drunk again, I'm going to kick you out-- even if it was [INAUDIBLE]. And sure enough when I came up to them and asked for a job, he said, show me what you can. Show me half a dozen garments. I showed him a half dozen. In a second he said, you got the job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your wife work here?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife worked, also. But first she didn't on account of my daughter. She had to stay home with my daughter for nine years. But she was nine years old, my daughter at the time. But then my wife started working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seamstress.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you both made a good living?

speaker: interviewee
text: We made a living Not a good living.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is your daughter now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter is born in '46. So that makes her what? This year she's going to be 48? Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is she now?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Amsterdam for the last seven days. She's coming back tomorrow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Visiting?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's not-- she-- yeah-- people living there we know. But she didn't go for that purpose. She had the chance to go for five days. She took three days' extension on her own, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you and your wife now retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see you have a number on your arm? Where was that number done?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came into Blechhammer on December 4, 1942, it was a work lager, they call it. On April 1, 1944, it became an SS lager. On April 2, 1944 I got my number.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: 178621. I lost my name at the time. I only had a number. Because I remember-- when I told you before I was a foreman all the time, when the German came by when I was with a cart, with the transportation what we did, I didn't-- I had to report to him not by, 'My name is Max Stodel. We're doing this and that.' But I had to report to him, 'My number is 178621.' I say it in English. 'I am doing that and that.' I never forget one day I was going too slow, because it was not enough time to make another transport. So I reported to him. And I said I'm number so-and-so. And he said you got to work a little faster. So I said, OK. And I tell the guy we've got to work a little faster. And I turned around to see if he was still there watching us. Sure enough, he was still there watching us. Called me back. And again, he gave me one of the face. He hit me in the face, because he said, yeah, you turned around to see if you're ever still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to your daughter. Is your daughter married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she is.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her married name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to spell it. L-A-Z-A-R-U-S. Lazarus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does she have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: She has one child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Philip Maxwell.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would you like Philip Maxwell to remember you by?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I was a nice grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never bored my-- neither my daughter, or my son-in-law, or my grandson, what happened to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Came naturally. Never on purpose, conscious or unconscious. We never brought it up at the dinner table, at the lunch table, breakfast table, when my daughter was still in the house. Like I said, conscious or unconscious, I don't know. But after, let's say, about 10 years ago, when my wife and I was talking to each other, we mentioned to each other that we never did bring it up. Never ever. It came naturally-- not-- not done conscious or unconsciously.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't your daughter ask you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter, when she grew up, she herself became involved with the second generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she didn't ask you about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: She figured, she might-- I can't read her mind-- if you didn't bring it up to me, why should I bring I up to you? Why shall I rub it in? My idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you doing this for your daughter or for your grandchild?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm doing it for the whole world, not for one person. For the future. Because what I-- what I put on that video, or whatever it is, is just a very minor portion of what you experience in 48 hours a day, from November 16, 1942 to April 30, finally when we settled in Amsterdam-- I settled in 1945. Because I can think of everything. That's why, if want to talk about one thing, another thing come to my mind. And if that other thing that come to my mind, brings another thing to my mind. And still I don't tell everything about the experience. Because I told you before there was a man died by the name of [NON-ENGLISH]-- he felt [INAUDIBLE], after the war, that the German soldier or whatever doctor was, came to tell me. When he-- he wasn't a very intelligent person. When I told him that I was getting illegal mail to those people that I mentioned before, he asked me if I could do something for him also. What I tried to do, didn't succeed.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were other people who asked me to do the same thing. Oh, I was in a camp with a boy by the name of Bukman-- was his name Bukman or Bugman? Bukman, I think. And he asked me to do something, also-- what I didn't succeed in. And right after the war, when I was with my sister-in-law living, some relative of that person came to the door and inquired to me what happened. Are you telling me that all these things are important to you, that future generations should know about?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your grandchild?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's one of the future also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything special you want him to know about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want him to know what I went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: What for? To make him miserable? If he picks it up himself, I'm very pleased. If he's not interested, I can't make him interested.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you raise your child and your grandchild in such a way that they would be interested?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't see the purpose of it. I can't force him if he's not interested. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If-- if a young man, you can put yourself in his place. He is-- he is right now, he's working with his studies. And he's interested in girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you know, we-- we can't be interested in something we don't know about. Maybe if we know about something, then we become interested.

speaker: interviewee
text: He know that we were in a concentration-- he know that we are Holocaust survivor. Why, he is 21 years old. So he's now come into maturity. He's 22, I think already.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can understand that when he was 12 years old he was not interested. He has his own life. Now, maybe, who knows? That next week, next month, next year he pops out and comes to me and say tell me everything that you went through. It's a possibility-- I hope so. Is it important to you that he knows anything about Judaism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody-- he's got bar mitzvah and everything. So he's a Jew. Right now I'm working on he doesn't smoke. He smokes. I'm not going to discuss with him about my being in the camp, and that he smoke. My-- smoking is more important to me for his health.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what message would you like to leave, a final message to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message I would like to leave? Somebody with a-- with a little bit of sense who can place himself what we Jews went through, that they put themself in a place to think about it, what we went through. Now, I forgot the whole-- a very important thing to you. You didn't ask me, of course, you didn't know. You asked me only questions what you might know. My wife, my second wife. I didn't have any children with my first wife. My second wife went into Auschwitz. When she got off the train, I wasn't there-- hearsay, from my wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: They asked for- they selected 100 married women. Why 100? And why 100, I don't know. But why married? Because they didn't want any virgins. And my wife was placed in a camp in Auschwitz in a block of 10.

speaker: interviewee
text: They did experiments on them, on my sister-in-law, and on my wife. You saw they were married to two brothers-- they had the same family name, and the maiden name. But the mix of us, don't ask me-- they were with Dr. Mengele.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I understand that we took testimonies, that we have your wife's story and your sister-in-law's story on tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I don't know if they mentioned in the tape that only a few got children. And my wife one of the few.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see the tape yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Max, I understand that you have a lot of interesting pictures that you want to show us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pictures and the-- the copies of the letters that I wrote to the Netherlands, wrote to my sister-in-law in the Netherlands. They are copies. And even they wrote me back, I corresponded with them three or four times, and I got the letters to prove it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we would like to show some of them on the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: You may.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture where you're looking at, on the left on the top is my second oldest sister. Her name is Eva. She was married with Van Buuren. So her name is Eva Van Buuren, and Stodel. And she was born in Amsterdam. And that picture-- and all the pictures as a matter of fact, what you're going to see, what you're seeing-- was taken around middle of '42. The next one is my father, Isaac Stodel. Next to him is his sister, who was living with us, as I told on the tape, Branca Stodel. Then my oldest sister, the last one on the top, Vrouwtje Stodel. She was married to Simon Schwitevoerder, who I met in Blechhammer. On the bottom on the left hand side, and my oldest brother, Jope, we know him, Stodel. His name was Josef. And in the middle is my youngest sister, Klara, [INAUDIBLE] Blitz, Stodel. In Amsterdam-- they're all born in Amsterdam. And she is the one who has an-- child who is still alive, is Maxwell Blitz, who lives in Canada. And I can tell you that she-- one day, I met one of the grandchildren that she doesn't know about, and I told that grandchild about her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next one is my youngest brother, Abraham Stodel. The left-hand child there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, the picture what you're looking at now is, on the left-hand side, is my second wife, Selien Stodel Karls. And then you see a lady in the middle. And she was-- and her husband--who took my nephew in, my-- youngest son of my youngest sister. And they did very good work for the Jewish people, because they helped more Jewish people. And they even got rewarded by the Israeli government.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name is-- she is Margaret Kiket. His name is Henry Kiket. I write them occasionally. Not too often-- maybe twice, three times a year. They took care of my nephew, who is standing on the bottom there, and the dark hair. His name is Maxwell Blitz, the one who lived in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one on the left side?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left side with the dark hair, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the two other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other two are also adopted children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not Jewish children, no. The picture where you looking at now is me when I was five, six, seven years old. Maybe six years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been taken in 1929 maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Amsterdam. Everything-- This picture, what you're looking at is that we went out from the school where they attended the Talmud Torah in 1936, when I was 13 years old. And last night, when I looked at the picture, what I do occasionally, I counted the people, the other children who survived. And I counted to the best of my knowledge that only four people who came back after the war. Now, from-- I know that from the four, one died-- that is the teacher lady Marie Boller. Her sister is on the right-hand side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am-- I am in the middle of the picture, with the short sleeve-- I'm the only one with the short sleeve. My sleeves are rolled to a short sleeve. There is only one more guy on the far left side who is wearing short sleeve, and his underwear is showing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you are-- you are in the middle? And what is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Max Stodel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [NON-ENGLISH], in the Netherlands. It's about-- This is me, Max Stodel there. You don't recognize him on account of the hair. The picture was taken in Amsterdam, and in the middle of '42, I would say. This is a picture looking at, was taken in Kremboong, in a work lager, war camp. And I am the second from the right. It was taken there about in I would say May. My name's Max Stodel. It was in the middle of May. No, no, no. May, or May, maybe, of 1942. And the place is Kremboong, as I mentioned before. And this is my first wife. Her name is Jeanette Van Bruun-- her name is Van Bruun. And that's taken in Amsterdam. And I think it was taken in I would say May of '42, because she-- we got married in that outfit that she's wearing. And I-- when she visit me in Kremboong one day, I asked her to come in the outfit that she was wearing the day that she got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was also killed in Auschwitz in-- I think it's Auschwitz, because the paper said so-- in November, 19-- 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same day. Just one second. Got it from a paper from the West Los Angeles College. It was taken about 12 years ago or so-- maybe a little longer-- 13 years ago. And since those sisters look so much alike, they were picked by the photographer and the-- to have the number on their arm taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right is my sister-in-law Klara Halberstadt. And on the left, closer to the column letters, is my wife, Selien Stodel Karls. And it was taken in West Los Angeles College in Culver City. And I think it was in the middle of the year 1983, or so. The picture you are looking at now is my daughter, Betty Sophia Lazarus Stodel, with her son Philip Maxwell Lazarus. And I think that picture was taken when my grandson was-- Philip Lazarus then four or five years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say '78 or so-- '79. And it was taken in Culver City, California. This is a picture of my grandson, Philip Maxwell Lazarus. And that when he graduated from high school. And I think it was taken in Culver City in the summer of '87, or so. I don't remember the year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which high school did he go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to Culver City high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
